topic: Someone finds out their medical records were mixed up with another patient's
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the hospital waiting room hummed with an irritating monotony. Elias stared at the printed summary in his hands, the paper feeling brittle beneath his fingertips. A cold knot tightened in his chest, a sensation he hadn't recognized until this moment. The dates, the diagnoses—they weren't his. His name was listed under a different set of identifiers, a stranger’s history smeared across his personal file. He traced a line of text with a shaky finger, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming in his ears. He pushed himself up from the hard plastic chair, the movement slow, deliberate, as if wading through thick water. Every glance toward the closed doors of the examination rooms felt like an accusation. He didn't speak; he simply tucked the papers into his worn messenger bag, the weight of the discovery settling heavily onto his shoulders, making every breath feel shallow.

[story 2]

"This can't be right." Sarah whispered, the words catching in her throat as she reread the discharge summary. The clinical jargon swam before her eyes, yet the reality hit with brutal clarity: these were her notes, her fragile health details, belonging to someone else entirely. A deep furrow settled between her brows, pulling the skin taut across her forehead. She gripped the edge of the desk until her knuckles were white, the small movement betraying the internal turmoil churning beneath her calm exterior. She didn't look up, focusing instead on a distant spot on the wall, letting the sheer injustice of the misfiling wash over her. The air in the small office seemed suddenly thick, suffocating.

[story 3]

He sat at the edge of the bench, the institutional beige of the hallway pressing in on him. The revelation had settled into a deep, heavy stillness within him, a weight that made his limbs feel sluggish. He replayed the administrative error in his mind—a simple clerical slip, yet it had unravelled something fundamental. His jaw was clenched so tightly he could feel the strain in his molars. When a nurse approached to ask if he needed assistance, he offered a curt nod, his gaze fixed on the scuff marks on the linoleum floor. His voice, when it finally emerged, was low and measured, devoid of inflection, conveying a profound sense of being utterly disconnected from the surrounding environment.
